---
title: "Interventions"
subtitle: "Composable interventions through the competing risks framework"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
engine: julia
---

## Overview

EpiBranch.jl models interventions through a **competing risks** framework.
The branching process generates potential contacts. Each contact's fate is
determined by competing risks: the generation time (when would transmission
occur?) vs the intervention time (when is the parent isolated/traced?).

This is grounded in survival analysis — the generation time CDF is the
survival function of remaining potential transmission, and isolation
truncates it.

```{julia}
using EpiBranch
using Distributions
using DataFrames
using StableRNGs
```

## How interventions work

### The three hooks

Every intervention can implement up to three methods:

1. **`initialise_individual!`** — set up fields on a new contact
   (e.g. Isolation adds `:isolated` and `:isolation_time`)
2. **`resolve_individual!`** — determine intervention state before
   transmission (e.g. compute isolation time from onset + delay)
3. **`apply_post_transmission!`** — act on contacts after creation
   (e.g. contact tracing identifies and traces contacts)

### The two universal modifiers

All interventions ultimately affect transmission through two numbers:

- **`susceptibility`** (on the contact): probability of being infected given
  exposure. Modified by vaccination, prior immunity.
- **`infectiousness`** (on the parent): modifier on onward transmission.
  Modified by isolation (via hazard truncation), treatment.

## Isolation

Isolation reduces transmission by truncating the infectious period.
How much it reduces depends on *when* isolation happens relative to
the generation time distribution:

```{julia}
model = BranchingProcess(Poisson(3.0), Exponential(5.0))

# Compare fast vs slow isolation
delays = [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0]

for d in delays
    iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(d))
    rng = StableRNG(42)
    results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
        interventions = [iso],
        sim_opts = SimOpts(
            max_cases = 500,
            incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5),
        ),
        rng = rng
    )
    cp = containment_probability(results)
    println("Isolation delay ~ Exp($(d)): containment = $(round(cp, digits=3))")
end
```

### Leaky isolation

Perfect isolation assumes zero transmission after isolation. In practice,
household contacts may still occur:

```{julia}
for r in [0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5]
    iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0), residual_transmission = r)
    rng = StableRNG(42)
    results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
        interventions = [iso],
        sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500, incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
        rng = rng
    )
    cp = containment_probability(results)
    println("Residual = $(r): containment = $(round(cp, digits=3))")
end
```

## Contact tracing

Contact tracing identifies contacts of isolated cases and can:
- Isolate them earlier (before symptom onset, if quarantined)
- Track effort (number of contacts traced)

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))

for p in [0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]
    ct = ContactTracing(probability = p, delay = Exponential(1.0))
    rng = StableRNG(42)
    results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
        interventions = [iso, ct],
        sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500, incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
        rng = rng
    )
    cp = containment_probability(results)
    println("Trace prob = $(p): containment = $(round(cp, digits=3))")
end
```

### Quarantine vs no quarantine

With quarantine, traced contacts can be isolated *before* symptom onset:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))
ct_q = ContactTracing(probability = 0.7, delay = Exponential(1.0), quarantine_on_trace = true)
ct_nq = ContactTracing(probability = 0.7, delay = Exponential(1.0), quarantine_on_trace = false)

rng1 = StableRNG(42)
r_q = simulate_batch(model, 200;
    interventions = [iso, ct_q],
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500, incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
    rng = rng1
)

rng2 = StableRNG(42)
r_nq = simulate_batch(model, 200;
    interventions = [iso, ct_nq],
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500, incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
    rng = rng2
)

println("With quarantine:    $(round(containment_probability(r_q), digits=3))")
println("Without quarantine: $(round(containment_probability(r_nq), digits=3))")
```

## Asymptomatic cases

Asymptomatic individuals escape symptom-based surveillance — they are
never isolated and their contacts are never traced:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))
ct = ContactTracing(probability = 0.5, delay = Exponential(1.0))

for pa in [0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5]
    rng = StableRNG(42)
    results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
        interventions = [iso, ct],
        sim_opts = SimOpts(
            max_cases = 500,
            incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5),
            prob_asymptomatic = pa,
        ),
        rng = rng
    )
    cp = containment_probability(results)
    println("$(Int(pa*100))% asymptomatic: containment = $(round(cp, digits=3))")
end
```

## Test sensitivity

Imperfect testing means some symptomatic cases are missed:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))

for ts in [0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0]
    rng = StableRNG(42)
    results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
        interventions = [iso],
        sim_opts = SimOpts(
            max_cases = 500,
            incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5),
            test_sensitivity = ts,
        ),
        rng = rng
    )
    cp = containment_probability(results)
    println("Test sensitivity = $(ts): containment = $(round(cp, digits=3))")
end
```

## Effort tracking

Because all contacts are stored (infected and non-infected), you can
compute intervention effort metrics:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))
ct = ContactTracing(probability = 0.7, delay = Exponential(1.0))

rng = StableRNG(42)
state = simulate_conditioned(
    BranchingProcess(NegBin(2.5, 0.16), Exponential(5.0)),
    50:200;
    interventions = [iso, ct],
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 200, incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
    rng = rng
)

total = length(state.individuals)
infected = count(is_infected, state.individuals)
traced = count(is_traced, state.individuals)
isolated = count(is_isolated, state.individuals)
quarantined = count(is_quarantined, state.individuals)

println("Effort summary:")
println("  Total contacts generated: ", total)
println("  Infections: ", infected)
println("  Traced: ", traced)
println("  Isolated: ", isolated)
println("  Quarantined: ", quarantined)
println("  Contacts per case: ", round(total / infected, digits = 1))
println("  Traces per case: ", round(traced / infected, digits = 1))
```

## Writing a custom intervention

Any struct that subtypes `AbstractIntervention` can be an intervention.
Here's a minimal example — a "super-spreader detection" intervention
that reduces infectiousness for high-offspring individuals:

```{julia}
struct SuperSpreaderDetection <: AbstractIntervention
    threshold::Int      # offspring above this triggers detection
    reduction::Float64  # infectiousness reduction when detected
end

# Initialise our field on each individual
function EpiBranch.initialise_individual!(ssd::SuperSpreaderDetection, ind, state)
    ind.state[:detected_superspreader] = false
end

# After transmission, check if the parent had many contacts
function EpiBranch.apply_post_transmission!(ssd::SuperSpreaderDetection, state, new_contacts)
    # Count contacts per parent
    parent_counts = Dict{Int, Int}()
    for c in new_contacts
        parent_counts[c.parent_id] = get(parent_counts, c.parent_id, 0) + 1
    end
    # Mark high-offspring parents
    for (pid, count) in parent_counts
        if count > ssd.threshold
            idx = findfirst(i -> i.id == pid, state.individuals)
            if idx !== nothing
                state.individuals[idx].state[:detected_superspreader] = true
                state.individuals[idx].infectiousness *= (1.0 - ssd.reduction)
            end
        end
    end
end
```

This custom intervention composes with the built-in ones:

```{julia}
model = BranchingProcess(NegBin(2.5, 0.16), Exponential(5.0))
ssd = SuperSpreaderDetection(5, 0.9)

rng = StableRNG(42)
state = simulate(model;
    interventions = [ssd],
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 200),
    rng = rng
)

n_detected = count(ind -> get(ind.state, :detected_superspreader, false), state.individuals)
println("Super-spreaders detected: ", n_detected)
```
